problem_addressed: Semi-supervised learning for medical image classification faces challenges with class imbalance due to scarcity of disease-positive individuals, which existing SSL methods don't adequately address.
core_claim: The Adaptive Blended Consistency Loss (ABCL) effectively counteracts data skew in imbalanced medical image classification by adaptively blending target class distributions based on class frequencies.
key_contributions: Proposed Adaptive Blended Consistency Loss (ABCL) as a drop-in replacement for consistency loss in perturbation-based SSL methods
Demonstrated improvements to unweighted average recall on two imbalanced medical image classification datasets
novelty: Unlike existing consistency losses (CL and SCL) that always target the original sample prediction (OSP) and don't account for class imbalance, ABCL adaptively blends OSP and augmented sample predictions (ASP) based on class frequencies to counteract bias toward majority classes.
method_summary: ABCL creates a blended target distribution that combines OSP and ASP predictions with weighting determined by class frequencies, pulling both original and augmented predictions toward this target distribution using Kullback-Leibler divergence loss.
method_components: ABCL loss function: L_ABCL(z, ŷ) = L_con(z_blended, z) + L_con(z_blended, ŷ)
Blended target distribution: z_blended = (1-k) * z + k * ŷ
Weighting calculation: k = max(0, min(γ * (N_original - N_augmented) + 0.5, 1))
key_innovation: Adaptive weighting of target distributions based on class frequencies to counteract natural bias toward majority classes, with k < 0.5 when OSP is minority class and k > 0.5 when ASP is minority class.
assumptions: The model's predictions for original and augmented samples can be meaningfully combined
Class frequency information can effectively guide the blending of target distributions
theoretical_grounding: Analysis of four prediction cases (Tab. 1) demonstrating how ABCL addresses limitations of CL and SCL by moving target distributions toward minority classes when predictions disagree, with no formal proofs provided.
metrics_used: ROC and AUC for each class
hyperparameters: Weak data augmentation (random horizontal flips, rotations 0-180°, erasing 0.02-0.33 proportion, color augmentation 0.9-1.1), γ=0.4 for ABCL, β=0.8 for SCL, Class weights [0.11,0.67,0.06,0.03,0.11,0.01,0.01] for HAM10000
headline_result: UDA-WeightedCE-ABCL achieved UAR of 0.68, G-mean of 0.66, and average AUC of 0.96 on HAM10000 dataset
ablations: Impact of combining WeightedCE with ABCL
improvement_over_baseline: ABCL improved UAR from 0.59 to 0.67 (+0.08), G-mean from 0.56 to 0.62 (+0.06), and average AUC from 0.91 to 0.95 (+0.04) over UDA baseline
best_result: UDA-WeightedCE-ABCL achieved UAR of 0.68, G-mean of 0.66, and average AUC of 0.96 on HAM10000 dataset
title: Semi-supervised learning for medical image classification using imbalanced training data
